findings.title: Admin password reset (POST /users/:id/reset-password) does not bump nbf — access token remains valid up to 7 days post-reset
findings.description: The admin endpoint POST /users/:id/reset-password revokes all refresh tokens but never calls jwtNotBefore.bumpUser(). SEC-019 relies on nbf bump to invalidate existing access tokens after a password change. In production, JWT_ACCESS_TTL is unset and JWT_EXPIRES_IN defaults to 7d (docker-compose.prod.yml:121 and .env.production.example:60), so the effective access token lifetime is 7 days. An access token stolen before the admin reset remains fully valid for up to 7 days after the admin changed the password — the primary defence scenario for admin resets. Self-service changePassword also omits bumpUser. Only AuthService.resetPassword (token-based flow) correctly calls bumpUser.
findings.root_cause: jwtNotBefore.bumpUser(userId) was added to deactivation paths (SEC-020) and the token-based reset flow (AUTH-019) but was forgotten in the admin reset endpoint and self-service changePassword.
findings.impact: An attacker who stole a valid access token before an admin reset retains full API access for up to 7 days in production. This defeats the primary goal of admin-forced password resets (credential rotation after compromise).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await this.jwtNotBefore.bumpUser(userId);` after `await this.refreshTokenService.revokeAllForUser(userId);` at line 1129 in users.service.ts (resetPassword). Also add to changePassword (around line 1046 after revokeAllForUser). Alternatively, define a private helper `async invalidateAllTokens(userId)` that calls both and use it consistently across all password-changing paths.
acceptance_criteria: After admin POST /users/:id/reset-password, any previously issued access token for that user receives 401 on the next request
After user PATCH /me/change-password, any previously issued access token for that user receives 401 on the next request
Integration test: issue token, reset password via admin endpoint, assert token returns 401
findings.notes: Production access TTL confirmed: docker-compose.prod.yml sets JWT_EXPIRES_IN=7d; auth.service.ts getAccessTtl() returns JWT_ACCESS_TTL||JWT_EXPIRES_IN||'15m'. No JWT_ACCESS_TTL is set in any prod template.

findings.title: Self-service changePassword (PATCH /me/change-password) does not bump nbf — old access tokens remain valid up to 7 days
findings.description: changePassword revokes all refresh tokens but never bumps the per-user nbf in Redis. An attacker who acquired the old access token (e.g., from a stolen localStorage on a shared device) continues to use it until it naturally expires. Under the production config where JWT_EXPIRES_IN=7d and JWT_ACCESS_TTL is unset, this window is 7 days. The nbf mechanism was specifically designed for this use case (SEC-019) but was not wired up to self-service password changes.
findings.root_cause: Same root cause as SEC-001: nbf bump not called after password credential rotation.
findings.impact: A user who changes their password to regain control of a compromised account (e.g., after noticing unauthorized activity) leaves the attacker's stolen access token valid for up to 7 days.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `await this.jwtNotBefore.bumpUser(userId);` after `await this.refreshTokenService.revokeAllForUser(userId);` at line ~1046 in users.service.ts (changePassword).
acceptance_criteria: After PATCH /me/change-password, any previously issued access token for that user returns 401
Integration test: issue token, change password, assert token returns 401 on next request

findings.title: LoginDto missing @MaxLength — unbounded login and password fields enable bcrypt DoS
findings.description: Both the login and password fields in LoginDto have no @MaxLength constraint. The POST /auth/login endpoint calls bcrypt.compare() on the submitted password. bcrypt with cost=12 is intentionally slow; submitting a multi-megabyte password string will block the event loop for seconds per attempt. The global bodyLimit of 1 MiB (main.ts) caps the body size but still permits strings up to ~1 MB which are expensive to hash. The per-IP throttle (5/min) slows this but does not prevent a targeted DoS across distributed source IPs.
findings.root_cause: LoginDto was never updated with @MaxLength constraints; only @MinLength was added.
findings.impact: An attacker can submit a very long password string to POST /auth/login, causing bcrypt to block the Node.js event loop for an extended period per request, potentially causing DoS. Even with throttling, a single request with a near-1MB password causes measurable latency.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@MaxLength(72)` to both the login and password fields in LoginDto. 72 bytes is bcrypt's internal limit; longer inputs are silently truncated by bcrypt anyway. Alternatively use @MaxLength(128) for login and @MaxLength(72) for password.
acceptance_criteria: POST /auth/login with a 10KB+ password returns 400 immediately without calling bcrypt
POST /auth/login with a 10KB+ login returns 400 immediately

findings.title: RefreshTokenDto missing @MaxLength — unbounded refresh token in POST /auth/refresh
findings.description: Both RefreshTokenDto.refreshToken and LogoutDto.refreshToken lack @MaxLength. The refresh flow passes this value to SHA-256 hashing, which is fast and unbounded. While not a bcrypt-level DoS risk, applying @MaxLength is a defence-in-depth measure that prevents surprisingly large payloads from even reaching the service layer. The refresh cookie path is already length-capped (cookies have browser limits) but the body path is not.
findings.root_cause: MaxLength constraint not applied to opaque token fields in auth DTOs.
findings.impact: Minor — SHA-256 on large strings is fast but wastes CPU. Not a significant DoS vector at the bodyLimit (1 MiB).
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@MaxLength(256)` to both refreshToken fields (a 48-byte base64url token is 64 chars; 256 provides generous headroom for variations).
acceptance_criteria: POST /auth/refresh with a 10KB refreshToken body field returns 400

findings.title: CreateCommentDto and UpdateCommentDto missing @MaxLength on content field
findings.description: The content field in CreateCommentDto and UpdateCommentDto has no @MaxLength constraint. An authenticated user can submit a very large comment payload that would be stored verbatim in the database, consuming disk space and memory when returned in comment listings. The global bodyLimit of 1 MiB limits individual requests but an attacker can fill the comments table with 1 MiB entries across multiple requests.
findings.root_cause: MaxLength constraint not applied to the comment content field.
findings.impact: Authenticated users can pollute the database with large comment entries, causing storage exhaustion and slow queries on task detail pages. Lower risk due to authentication requirement.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `@MaxLength(10000)` (or a domain-appropriate limit) to the content field in both CreateCommentDto and UpdateCommentDto.
acceptance_criteria: POST /comments with a content > 10000 chars returns 400
PATCH /comments/:id with a content > 10000 chars returns 400

findings.title: CreateProjectDto.description, icon, managerId, sponsorId missing @MaxLength
findings.description: Four fields in CreateProjectDto lack @MaxLength: description (free text, can be arbitrarily large), icon (emoji or string), managerId and sponsorId (should be @IsUUID, not @IsString). The description and icon fields can receive arbitrarily long strings up to the 1 MiB body limit and be persisted in the database. The managerId and sponsorId fields accept any string rather than a UUID, potentially causing unexpected Prisma behavior on invalid values.
findings.root_cause: MaxLength and UUID validation were not applied to optional fields in CreateProjectDto.
findings.impact: Authenticated users with projects:create permission can store large project descriptions and icons. managerId/sponsorId accept arbitrary strings which Prisma may reject at the DB level with a 500 rather than a clean 400.
findings.suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength to description (e.g., 5000 chars), icon (e.g., 10 chars for emoji), and replace @IsString() with @IsUUID('4') for managerId and sponsorId.
acceptance_criteria: POST /projects with description > 5000 chars returns 400
POST /projects with managerId = 'not-a-uuid' returns 400

findings.title: CreateTaskDto.description and tags fields missing @MaxLength
findings.description: The description field in CreateTaskDto has no @MaxLength. Similarly, the tags array field has no per-item or total-count limit. Large task descriptions are stored in the database and returned in list responses, potentially causing memory and bandwidth issues. The tags array could be used to store large amounts of data.
findings.root_cause: MaxLength/ArrayMaxSize constraints were not applied to optional free-text and array fields in CreateTaskDto.
findings.impact: Authenticated users can create tasks with very large descriptions, impacting database storage and API response sizes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength(10000) to description. Add @ArrayMaxSize(20) and @MaxLength(50, { each: true }) to tags.
acceptance_criteria: POST /tasks with description > 10000 chars returns 400
POST /tasks with tags containing more than 20 items returns 400

findings.title: CreateLeaveDto.reason and targetUserId missing @MaxLength and @IsUUID
findings.description: The reason field in CreateLeaveDto has no @MaxLength, allowing large text storage in leave records. The targetUserId field uses @IsString() instead of @IsUUID(), accepting arbitrary strings instead of enforcing the UUID format documented in the example. A non-UUID targetUserId would cause a Prisma error at the DB layer rather than a clean 400 validation error.
findings.root_cause: MaxLength and @IsUUID not applied to leave creation DTO.
findings.impact: Large reason strings stored in leave records; non-UUID targetUserId causes unhandled Prisma errors returned as 500.
findings.suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength(2000) to reason. Replace @IsString() with @IsUUID('4') on targetUserId.
acceptance_criteria: POST /leaves with reason > 2000 chars returns 400
POST /leaves with targetUserId = 'not-a-uuid' returns 400

findings.title: CreateLeaveDto.leaveTypeId uses @IsString() instead of @IsUUID()
findings.description: The leaveTypeId field is documented as a UUID-like identifier but validated only with @IsString(). The example 'lt-cp-001' suggests this may intentionally be a non-UUID short identifier; however, if leaveTypeId is a database UUID FK (as the schema likely defines), only @IsUUID() is appropriate. If it really is a non-UUID slug, then it should have a @MaxLength and @Matches constraint. Either way, unbounded @IsString() is insufficient.
findings.root_cause: leaveTypeId validation not aligned with the actual data type used in the database schema.
findings.impact: A long leaveTypeId causes a Prisma findUnique miss (404) at worst, but could also cause slow regex/DB lookups with pathological inputs.
findings.suggested_fix: If leaveTypeId is a UUID FK in Prisma schema: replace @IsString()/@IsNotEmpty() with @IsUUID('4'). If it is a slug: add @MaxLength(64) @Matches(/^[a-z0-9-]+$/) or similar.
acceptance_criteria: POST /leaves with leaveTypeId of invalid format returns 400 instead of 404
findings.notes: Need to verify leaveTypeId DB type in schema.prisma to confirm whether it should be UUID or slug.

findings.title: CreateEpicDto.description and CreateMilestoneDto.description missing @MaxLength
findings.description: The description fields in CreateEpicDto and CreateMilestoneDto have no @MaxLength constraint. While CreateMilestoneDto similarly lacks it, this applies to all major resource DTOs where optional free-text description fields are not length-capped. Storage abuse by authenticated users is the primary risk.
findings.root_cause: Pattern of omitting @MaxLength on optional description fields across multiple DTOs.
findings.impact: Authenticated users can store large descriptions in epics and milestones, consuming database storage.
findings.suggested_fix: Add @MaxLength(5000) to description in CreateEpicDto and CreateMilestoneDto.
acceptance_criteria: POST /epics with description > 5000 chars returns 400
POST /milestones with description > 5000 chars returns 400

findings.title: Metrics endpoint token comparison uses !== (timing-unsafe) instead of timingSafeEqual
findings.description: The METRICS_TOKEN comparison uses the JavaScript !== operator, which is a timing-unsafe string comparison. This allows a timing side-channel attack to oracle the token value character-by-character. The Swagger basic-auth in main.ts uses timingSafeEqual (main.ts:37) but this pattern was not applied here. Although METRICS_TOKEN is a static secret and HTTPS in production makes timing attacks very difficult, consistency with the existing safeEqual() helper is appropriate.
findings.root_cause: The safeEqual() helper defined in main.ts (line 34-38) was not reused in the metrics controller.
findings.impact: Theoretical timing side-channel to brute-force the METRICS_TOKEN value. Practically negligible over HTTPS, but violates defence-in-depth.
findings.suggested_fix: Refactor the comparison to use crypto.timingSafeEqual: `if (!authorization || !timingSafeEqual(Buffer.from(authorization), Buffer.from(expected))) { throw ... }`. Extract safeEqual() from main.ts into a shared utility.
acceptance_criteria: METRICS_TOKEN comparison uses timingSafeEqual instead of !==

findings.title: METRICS_TOKEN not documented in env templates — metrics endpoint is open by default
findings.code_evidence:
/**
 * OBS-011 — Exposes /api/metrics in Prometheus text format.
 *
 * Auth logic:
 *  - If METRICS_TOKEN is unset (dev/test), the endpoint is fully open.
 *  - If METRICS_TOKEN is set, the request MUST include an Authorization header
 *    matching "Bearer <METRICS_TOKEN>". Otherwise → 401.
 *
 * We mark the controller @Public() to bypass the global JwtAuthGuard
 */
findings.description: METRICS_TOKEN is referenced in the controller but absent from both .env.example and .env.production.example. An operator following the env templates to configure production will not set METRICS_TOKEN, leaving /api/metrics fully unauthenticated. The endpoint exposes HTTP request counts, routes, status codes, and latencies — aggregated telemetry that reveals the application's internal route structure and error rates to any unauthenticated requester. This is also not protected by the global JwtAuthGuard due to @Public().
findings.root_cause: METRICS_TOKEN was added to the controller but not to the env template files used for operator onboarding.
findings.impact: Unauthenticated access to production telemetry: route enumeration, traffic patterns, error rate inference. Low impact on its own but adds to reconnaissance capability.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `METRICS_TOKEN=` (with a generation command) to .env.example and .env.production.example with a prominent note that it is required in production. Also add to docker-compose.prod.yml's API environment block.
acceptance_criteria: METRICS_TOKEN is documented in .env.example
METRICS_TOKEN is documented in .env.production.example
GET /api/metrics returns 401 in production when no token is provided

findings.title: Nginx config has no HSTS header and no HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect
findings.description: The nginx config defines only a port 80 server block with no HTTPS listener, no HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect, and no Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header. The comment 'behind host nginx SSL proxy' suggests TLS may terminate upstream, but the shipped nginx.conf would serve production traffic over plain HTTP if deployed without an additional TLS-terminating proxy. Even if TLS terminates upstream, HSTS should be emitted by the application to instruct browsers to enforce HTTPS for future visits. Without HSTS, browsers will happily follow HTTP links to the application.
findings.root_cause: TLS configuration was deferred to an upstream proxy (host nginx/HAProxy), and no HSTS header was added to compensate.
findings.impact: If deployed without an upstream TLS proxy, all traffic including JWT access tokens (in Authorization headers) and refresh cookies transmit over cleartext HTTP. Even with upstream TLS, absence of HSTS leaves users vulnerable to SSL stripping on first visit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains" always;` to the server block. If TLS terminates upstream, add a dedicated HTTPS server block with SSL certs or redirect port 80 to 443 within the Docker stack.
acceptance_criteria: curl -I https://prod-host/ includes Strict-Transport-Security header
HTTP requests to port 80 are redirected to HTTPS (301)
findings.notes: Architecture comment 'behind host nginx SSL proxy' implies TLS may be handled externally; this finding assumes the shipped nginx.conf is the complete deployment config. Impact is blocking if nginx.conf is the sole ingress.

findings.title: docker-compose.prod.yml uses deprecated ALLOWED_ORIGINS env var instead of canonical CORS_ORIGIN
findings.description: The production compose file passes ALLOWED_ORIGINS to the API container, but the canonical variable name is CORS_ORIGIN as documented in .env.production.example and cors.config.ts (which reads CORS_ORIGIN first, ALLOWED_ORIGINS as deprecated alias). An operator who sets only CORS_ORIGIN (following .env.production.example instructions) will trigger the required-boot assertion error because docker-compose.prod.yml demands ALLOWED_ORIGINS be set. This creates a confusing, inconsistent operator interface.
findings.root_cause: docker-compose.prod.yml was not updated when CORS_ORIGIN was established as the canonical variable in SEC-012.
findings.impact: Operator confusion: the .env.production.example says CORS_ORIGIN, the compose file says ALLOWED_ORIGINS. No security bypass (cors.config.ts handles both), but a misconfigured deployment could use an unintended CORS origin list.
findings.suggested_fix: Update docker-compose.prod.yml line 130 to: `CORS_ORIGIN: ${CORS_ORIGIN:-${ALLOWED_ORIGINS}}` to accept both, or migrate exclusively to `CORS_ORIGIN: ${CORS_ORIGIN:?CORS_ORIGIN is required}`.
acceptance_criteria: docker-compose.prod.yml uses CORS_ORIGIN as the primary variable name

findings.title: X-Forwarded-Proto forwarded as $http_x_forwarded_proto without sanitization — spoofable by external client
findings.description: The nginx config sets X-Forwarded-Proto to $http_x_forwarded_proto, which is the value of the incoming X-Forwarded-Proto request header from the client. If a client sends a forged X-Forwarded-Proto: https header on an HTTP request, nginx will forward it unchanged to the API. This is harmless if the API does not make security decisions based on X-Forwarded-Proto, but should be $scheme (the actual protocol nginx received the request on) to prevent proto-header injection.
findings.root_cause: nginx was configured to forward client-supplied headers rather than using the nginx-determined $scheme variable.
findings.impact: Low — the API appears to use X-Forwarded-Proto primarily for logging. However if any future middleware logic (e.g., HTTPS enforcement checks) relies on this header, a client could spoof it to appear as HTTPS while communicating over HTTP.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $http_x_forwarded_proto;` to `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;` in both /api and / location blocks.
acceptance_criteria: nginx forwards X-Forwarded-Proto: http for plain HTTP requests regardless of client-supplied headers

findings.title: CI workflow hardcodes DB password and JWT secret in plaintext — appropriate for ephemeral CI but not using GitHub Secrets
findings.code_evidence:
postgres:
        image: postgres:18
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: orchestr_a
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: orchestr_a_dev_password
          POSTGRES_DB: orchestr_a_v2_test
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
findings.description: The CI workflow hardcodes `orchestr_a_dev_password` as the Postgres password and `test-jwt-secret-key` as the JWT secret in plaintext in the YAML file. While these are ephemeral CI values used only for test services, they are committed to the repository and will appear in git history indefinitely. If an operator ever reuses these values in a staging or production environment, those credentials are publicly known. The docker-publish.yml and docker-compose.yml also use the same default password.
findings.root_cause: CI DB credentials were not parameterized as GitHub Secrets, likely because they are considered dev-only throwaway values.
findings.impact: Directly: none — these are ephemeral CI containers. Indirectly: if an operator copies these values for convenience into a non-CI environment, those environments are compromised. The password `orchestr_a_dev_password` is a known weak default.
findings.suggested_fix: Move CI DB passwords to GitHub repository secrets (`${{ secrets.CI_DB_PASSWORD }}`) to establish a good hygiene pattern. Alternatively, document clearly that these values must NEVER be reused outside ephemeral CI.
acceptance_criteria: No plaintext passwords appear in ci.yml (use ${{ secrets.CI_DB_PASSWORD }} or similar)
findings.notes: The .env files containing real dev secrets are correctly gitignored. This finding applies only to the CI workflow YAML file itself.

findings.title: AppController GET / exposes docs and internal API endpoint list publicly without auth
findings.description: The root GET / endpoint is @Public() and returns the application name, version, status, and a structured list of key API endpoints including the path to the Swagger docs (/api/docs). This is an information-disclosure endpoint that aids reconnaissance. The Swagger endpoint path leakage is especially relevant if SWAGGER_ENABLED=true is ever set without noticing this public disclosure point. The version field (2.0.0) helps attackers correlate known vulnerabilities.
findings.root_cause: Root endpoint was designed for developer convenience with no auth and includes rich metadata.
findings.impact: Enables endpoint discovery and version fingerprinting without authentication. Low direct impact but reduces the work required for targeted attacks.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `endpoints` object and `version` from the root response, or restrict the endpoint to authenticated requests. A minimal `{ status: 'operational' }` response is sufficient for health discovery while minimizing information disclosure.
acceptance_criteria: GET / does not return version information or endpoint listing
GET /api/health continues to function for container health checks

findings.title: JWT_ACCESS_TTL undocumented in prod template — effective access token TTL is 7d not 15m as SEC-019 assumes
findings.description: The .env.production.example sets JWT_EXPIRES_IN=7d without defining JWT_ACCESS_TTL. The auth.service.ts getAccessTtl() function resolves: JWT_ACCESS_TTL || JWT_EXPIRES_IN || '15m'. In production, this resolves to 7d as the access token TTL. Several security code comments assert '15min access TTL' (e.g., jwt-not-before.service.ts:33: 'the 15-min access TTL caps the residual exposure') — those comments are incorrect for the default production config. The security model of SEC-001/SEC-002 (and the nbf fail-open logic) assumes short-lived access tokens; with 7d TTLs the fail-open on Redis error means tokens are valid for 7 days even if Redis is down.
findings.root_cause: JWT_ACCESS_TTL was added as a more granular control but not set in the production template; JWT_EXPIRES_IN was intended as the refresh token TTL but doubles as the access token TTL due to the fallback chain.
findings.impact: The effective production access token TTL is 7 days, not 15 minutes. The nbf fail-open window is 7 days instead of 15 minutes. The SEC-001/SEC-002 findings above describe consequences of stolen tokens with this 7d window.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `JWT_ACCESS_TTL=15m` (or 1h for a balance) to .env.production.example and docker-compose.prod.yml, separate from JWT_REFRESH_TTL=7d. Update code comments referencing the '15min' assumption.
acceptance_criteria: JWT_ACCESS_TTL is set independently in .env.production.example
JWT_EXPIRES_IN is used solely as the refresh token TTL or removed in favour of JWT_REFRESH_TTL
findings.notes: docker-compose.prod.yml:121 confirms: JWT_EXPIRES_IN: ${JWT_EXPIRES_IN:-7d}. auth.module.ts uses the same fallback chain. No JWT_ACCESS_TTL appears in any prod template.

verified_clean: Global RBAC guard is fail-closed: rbac.module.ts:43 registers PermissionsGuardV2 as APP_GUARD with default mode 'enforce' — routes without @Public/@AllowSelfService/@RequirePermissions are refused (not just logged). Production boot asserts RBAC_GUARD_MODE != permissive.

No user-controlled SQL injection: all queryRawUnsafe calls in scripts/normalize-action-codes.ts and scripts/recompute-chain-on-schema-bump.ts use only hardcoded constant trigger name strings (IMMUTABILITY_TRIGGER = 'audit_logs_no_update_delete'), never interpolated user input. All application queries use Prisma parameterized ORM calls.

Avatar file upload: MIME allowlist + assertMagicBytes() (file-type library) validates actual file magic bytes against ['image/png','image/jpeg','image/webp']. File path reconstructed from userId+extension (server-controlled), never from DB-stored URL. Path traversal guard with resolve()+sep prefix check in removeAvatarFiles().

Refresh token cookie hardening: __Host-orchestr_a_refresh_token in production, with HttpOnly + SameSite=Strict + Secure + Path=/ + Max-Age. Falls back to non-prefixed name only in non-prod environments where Secure cookies cannot be set on localhost.

JWT blacklist is fail-CLOSED: JwtBlacklistService.isBlacklisted() returns true on Redis error (line 65), preventing use of potentially revoked tokens on Redis failure. Blacklist write is fail-CLOSED too (throws 503 so client retries rather than getting false 204).

Login user enumeration neutralized (SEC-005): auth.service.ts:101 checks isActive after bcrypt.compare(), returning null in both invalid-password and inactive-account cases — same generic 401 and same timing for both failure modes.

CORS production boot-assert: cors.config.ts:39-44 throws if NODE_ENV=production and neither CORS_ORIGIN nor ALLOWED_ORIGINS is set. CORS correctly restricted to configured origin list, never wildcard.
Request body size limit: main.ts:98 sets bodyLimit:1048576 (1 MiB) on the FastifyAdapter, preventing unbounded JSON bodies from reaching validation.

Docker containers run as non-root: API Dockerfile creates nestjs user (UID 1001) and switches USER nestjs before EXPOSE. Web Dockerfile similarly uses nextjs user. Both prod compose services have security_opt: no-new-privileges and cap_drop: ALL.
Document URL field is scheme-allowlisted: CreateDocumentDto.url uses @IsUrl({protocols:['http','https'],require_protocol:true,require_tld:true}) blocking javascript:/data:/file: schemes (SEC-009).

Avatar URL stored-XSS prevention: CreateUserDto.avatarUrl validated with @Matches(AVATAR_URL_PATTERN) restricting to /api/uploads/avatars/<alphanumeric>.<ext> — no external hosts, no javascript: or data: schemes, no path traversal (SEC-010).
ValidationPipe is global with whitelist:true and forbidNonWhitelisted:true — extra fields in request bodies are stripped and rejected.

Secret boot-time assertions: assertJwtSecretStrength (SEC-026) refuses to boot in production if JWT_SECRET < 32 chars. assertAuditHashKey (OBS-028) refuses to boot in any environment without AUDIT_HASH_KEY. AUTH_EXPOSE_RESET_TOKEN=true forbidden in production (SEC-018). RBAC_GUARD_MODE=permissive forbidden in production (SEC-001).
Password hashing: bcrypt with cost factor 12, used consistently across create/update/reset paths. No password ever stored in plaintext or logs.

PII in audit logs: attempted login identifiers are HMAC-keyed with AUDIT_HASH_KEY before storage (OBS-028/SEC-019). LOG_LEVEL redaction config strips authorization headers, cookies, password fields, refresh tokens from Fastify access logs.
JWT blacklist + nbf gate on every authenticated request: JwtStrategy.validate() checks jti blacklist (fail-closed) and per-user nbf in sequence before returning the user object.

Rate limiting on sensitive endpoints: POST /auth/login throttled at 5/min + 120/15min. POST /auth/reset-password throttled at 5/min. POST /auth/register throttled at 30/min. Global ThrottlerModule provides baseline limits.
Static file uploads require authentication: uploads-auth.hook.ts Fastify onRequest hook enforces Bearer token on all /api/uploads/* paths, preventing anonymous avatar enumeration (SEC-016).

TestingModule (POST /testing/reset) excluded from production: app.module.ts:95 conditionally includes TestingModule only when NODE_ENV != 'production'. The controller also has a runtime production guard as defence-in-depth.

Registration is closed by default: REGISTRATION_ENABLED=false in both env templates. The register endpoint returns 403 unless REGISTRATION_ENABLED=true is explicitly set. Optional domain allowlist via REGISTRATION_EMAIL_DOMAIN.

.env files are gitignored: .gitignore:20 excludes .env; git ls-files confirms apps/api/.env, packages/database/.env and root .env are not committed. Only .env.example and .env.production.example (with empty secret values) are tracked.
